items.myth: E[X] is the value you should expect on the next single trial.
items.reality: E[X] is a long-run average over many independent repetitions; a single draw can be far from the mean, especially when variance is large.
items.example: A fair die has E[X] = 3.5, but each roll is an integer 1–6—never 3.5. One roll being 6 does not contradict the expectation.

items.myth: If E[X] = E[Y], then X and Y have the same distribution.
items.reality: Many different distributions share the same mean; variance, skew, and tail behavior distinguish them.
items.example: Bernoulli(0.5) and Uniform{0, 1} both have mean 0.5, but one is two-point and the other is continuous on [0, 1].

items.myth: Zero variance means the random variable is always zero.
items.reality: Var(X) = 0 means X is constant almost surely—it can equal any fixed value c, not necessarily 0.
items.example: If X always equals 7, E[X] = 7 and Var(X) = 0.

items.reality: E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y] holds regardless of dependence; independence is needed for factoring expectations of products, not sums.
items.example: Let Y = X always. E[X + Y] = E[2X] = 2E[X] = E[X] + E[Y] even though X and Y are perfectly dependent.
